glossary.def: A new feature formed by combining two or more inputs (most commonly the product x1·x2) so that a model can express effects in which one variable's influence depends on another. A single interaction term can make XOR-like data, which no straight line can separate, linearly separable.

Augmenting the inputs with all monomials up to a chosen degree d — squares, cubes and cross-terms — to let a linear model fit curvature. The term count grows as the binomial coefficient C(n+d, d), so the expansion buys flexibility at the cost of dimensionality.

Representing a periodic quantity (hour, day-of-week, month) as a sine/cosine pair, sin(2πt/P) and cos(2πt/P), so that the end of the cycle sits next to its start. It removes the false discontinuity a raw integer encoding places between, e.g., hour 23 and hour 0.

Term frequency × inverse document frequency: a text feature that weights each term by how often it appears in a document and down-weights terms common across the whole corpus. It is a cheap, interpretable baseline that distinctive words dominate and ubiquitous words barely affect.

A wrapper selection method that repeatedly fits the model, drops the feature the refit model ranks least important, and refits, until the desired number remains. Recomputing importances after each removal lets it keep features that only become useful once their redundant partners are gone.

Linear regression with a penalty proportional to the sum of absolute coefficients (λΣ|βj|). The corners of the L1 constraint region drive many coefficients to exactly zero, so the fit performs feature selection and estimation in one optimization; L2 (ridge) only shrinks, never zeros.

VIF_j = 1/(1 − R²_j), where R²_j is the R² from regressing feature x_j on all the other features. It is the factor by which collinearity inflates that coefficient's variance; values above 5 warrant a look and above 10 signal serious multicollinearity. At R²=0.8, VIF=5.

The upward bias in a performance estimate that occurs when features are chosen using the entire dataset before cross-validation, leaking the test folds' labels into the selection. With p≫n it can fake high accuracy on pure noise; nested cross-validation, which selects inside each fold, removes it.

refs.cite: Guyon, I. & Elisseeff, A. (2003). An Introduction to Variable and Feature Selection. Journal of Machine Learning Research 3.
Kuhn, M. & Johnson, K. (2019). Feature Engineering and Selection: A Practical Approach for Predictive Models. CRC Press.
Tibshirani, R. (1996). Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society B 58(1).
Ambroise, C. & McLachlan, G. J. (2002). Selection Bias in Gene Extraction on the Basis of Microarray Gene-Expression Data. PNAS 99(10).
Guyon, I., Weston, J., Barnhill, S. & Vapnik, V. (2002). Gene Selection for Cancer Classification using Support Vector Machines. Machine Learning 46.
Zou, H. & Hastie, T. (2005). Regularization and Variable Selection via the Elastic Net. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society B 67(2).
Kraskov, A., Stögbauer, H. & Grassberger, P. (2004). Estimating Mutual Information. Physical Review E 69(6).
